verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he went into the synagogue, and spake boldly for the space of three months, disputing and persuading the things concerning the kingdom of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when divers were hardened, and believed not, but spake evil of that way before the multitude, he departed from them, and separated the disciples, disputing daily in the school of one Tyrannus.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (a) See him in every place forcing his way into the synagogue, and in this manner departing thence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in every place, he wished to have the occasion given him by them. (c) He wished to separate the disciples thence, and to have the beginning for ceasing to assemble with them, given by (the Jews) themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it was not for nothing that he did this (b) which I have said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was henceforth “provoking them to jealousy.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For both the Gentiles readily received him, and the Jews, upon the Gentiles receiving him, repented. (a) This is why he continually made a stir among them, “for three months arguing and persuading concerning the kingdom of God:” for you must not suppose because you hear of his “speaking boldly,” that there was any harshness: it was of good things that he discoursed, of a kingdom: who would not have heard him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But when divers were hardened, speaking evil of the way.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They might well call it “the way;” this was indeed the way, that led into the kingdom of heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He departed from them, and separated the disciples, disputing daily in the school of one Tyrannus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this was done for the space of two years, so that all that were in Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10.) (a) Do you mark how much was effected by his persisting?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Both Jews and Greeks heard: (c) all that dwelt in Asia:” it was for this also that the Lord suffered him not to go into Asia (ch. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6) (on a former occasion); waiting, as it seems to me, for this same conjuncture.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Hom. xl. p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 245.) (b) “And God wrought special miracles by the hands of Paul: so that from his body were brought unto the sick handkerchiefs or aprons, and the diseases departed from them, and the evil spirits went out of them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not touched the wearer only (and so were healed), but also receiving them, they laid them upon the sick (and so healed them). (g) “He that believeth on Me,” saith Christ, “doeth greater works than those which I do.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This, and the miracle of the shadows is what He meant (in those words). (d) “Then certain of the vagabond Jews, exorcists, took upon them to call over them which had evil spirits the name of the Lord Jesus, saying, We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So entirely did they do all by way of trade!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe: vagabond, or, itinerant, Jewish exorcists.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And to believe indeed, they had no mind; but by that Name they wished to cast out the demons.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “By Jesus, whom Paul preacheth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Only see what a name Paul had got!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And there were seven sons of one Sceva, a Jew, and chief of the priests, which did so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the evil spirit answered and said, Jesus I know, and Paul I know; but who are ye?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the man in whom the evil spirit was leaped on them, and overcame them, and prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did it in secret: then their impotence is publicly exposed. (f) Then not the Name does anything, unless it be spoken with faith. (h) See how they used their weapons against themselves! (j) So far were they from thinking Jesus to be anything great: no, they must needs add Paul, as thinking him to be something great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here one may marvel how it was that the demon did not cooperate with the imposture of the exorcists, but on the contrary exposed them, and laid open their stage-play.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He seems to me (to have done this) in exceeding wrath: just as it might be, if a person being in uttermost peril, should be exposed by some pitiful creature, and wish to vent all his rage upon him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Jesus I know, and Paul I know.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, that there may not seem to be any slight put upon the Name of Jesus, (the demon) first confesses (Him), and then has permission given him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, to show that it was not any weakness of the Name, but all owing to the imposture of those men, why did not the same take place in the case of Paul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They fled out of that house naked and wounded:” he sorely battered their heads, perhaps rent their garments. (e) “And this became known to all, both Jews and Greeks, that dwelt at Ephesus, and fear fell upon them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And many of them that had believed came confessing and making known their practices.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For since they had got to possess such power as, by means of the demons, to do such things, well might this be the consequence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And many of them that practised curious arts, brought their books together, and burnt them in the presence of all men;”—having seen that there was no more use of them now that the demons themselves do these things—“and reckoned up the price of them, and found the amount fifty thousand pieces of silver.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So mightily grew the word of God and prevailed.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19, 20.) (i) “And” (so) “he disputed,” in the school of one Tyrannus for two years:” where were believers, and believers exceedingly (advanced in the faith).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover (Paul) writes (to them) as to great men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.) (b) “And having entered in to the synagogue,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why ἐπαρρησιάζετο?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It means, he was ready to confront dangers, and disputed more openly, not veiling the doctrines. (a) “But when some were hardened, and spake evil of the way, having departed from them, he separated the disciples.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He put a stop, it means, to their evil-speaking: he did not wish to kindle their envy, nor to bring them into more contention. (c) Hence let us also learn not to put ourselves in the way of evil-speaking men, but to depart from them: he did not speak evil, when himself evil spoken of.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He disputed daily,” and by this gained the many, that, being evil intreated and (evil) spoken of, he did not (utterly) break away from them, and keep aloof. (e) The evil-speakers are defeated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They calumniated the doctrine itself; (therefore) so as neither to rouse the disciples to wrath, nor * * them, he withdrew, showing that everywhere alike they repel salvation from them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here now he does not even apologize, seeing that the Gentiles everywhere have believed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In the school of one Tyrannus:” it was not that he sought the place, but without more ado where there was a school (there he discoursed). (d) And look, no sooner is the trial from those without over, than this from the demons begins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the infatuated Jewish hardness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having seen his garments working miracles, they paid no heed to it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What could be greater than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, on the contrary, it resulted in just the opposite effect.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If any of the heathens believe not, having seen the (very) dust working these effects, let him believe. (f) Wonderful, how great the power of them that have believed!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both Simon for the sake of merchandise sought the grace of the Spirit, and these for this object did this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What hardness (of heart)!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why does not Paul rebuke them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It would have looked like envy, therefore it is so ordered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This same took place in the case of Christ (Mark ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36): but then the person is not hindered, for it was the beginning of the new state of things: since Judas also is not hindered, whereas Ananias and Sapphira were struck dead: and many Jews even for opposing (Christ) suffered nothing, while Elymas was blinded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For I am not come,” saith Christ, “to judge the world, but that the world might be saved.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And seven sons,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See the villany of the men!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They still continued to be Jews, while wishing to make a gain of that Name.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All that they did was for glory and profit. (g) Look, in every case, how men are converted not so much in consequence of good things as of things fearful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the case of Sapphira, fear fell upon the Church, and men dared not join themselves to them: here they received handkerchiefs and aprons, and were healed: and after this, then they came confessing their sins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Hereby) the power of the demons is shown to be a great one, when it is against unbelievers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For why did he not say, “Who is Jesus?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was afraid, lest he also should suffer punishment; but, that it might be permitted him to take revenge upon those who mocked him, he did this; “Jesus,” says he, “I know,” etc. He was in dread of Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For why did not those wretched men say to him, We believe?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How much more splendid an appearance they would have made had they said this, that is, if they had claimed Him as their Master?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But instead of that, they spoke even those senseless words, “By Jesus, whom Paul preacheth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark the forbearance (of the writer), how he writes history and does not call names?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This makes the Apostles admirable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the evil spirit,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15), for what had happened at Philippi (ch. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 16) had given a lesson to these also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He mentions the name, and the number, thereby giving to the persons then living a credible proof of what he wrote.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why were they itinerant?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the sake of merchandise: not assuredly to bear tidings of the word; how should that be their object?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how ran they anon, preaching by the things they suffered?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Insomuch,” it says, “that all that dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ought not this to have converted all?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And marvel not, for nothing convinces malice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But come now, let us look at the affair of the exorcists, with what an evil disposition (they acted).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why the same was not done in the case of Christ, is an inquiry for another time, and not for the present, save that this also was well and usefully ordered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me that they did this also in mockery, and that in consequence of this (punishment), none dared even at random to name that Name.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did this put them upon confession?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because this was a most mighty argument of God’s omniscience (therefore), before they should be exposed by the demons, they accused themselves, fearing lest they should suffer the same things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when the demons their helpers are their accusers, what hope is there thenceforth, save the confession by deeds?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But see, I pray you, after such signs had been wrought, what evils within a short space ensue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such is human nature: it soon forgets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or, do ye not remember what has been the case among ourselves?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did not God last year shake our whole city?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did not all run to baptism?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did not whoremongers and effeminate and corrupt persons leave their dwellings, and the places where they spent their time, and change and become religious?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But three days passed, and they returned again to their own proper wickedness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And whence is this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From the excessive laziness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what marvel if, when the things have passed away (this be the case), seeing that, the images lasting perpetually, the result is such?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The fate of Sodom—say, does it not still last (in its effects)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well, did the dwellers beside it become any the better?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what say you to the son of Noah?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was he not such (as he is represented), did he not see with his eyes so vast a desolation, and yet was wicked?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not marvel how, when such things had been done, these Jews (at Ephesus) believe not, when we see that belief itself often comes round for them into its opposite, into malignity; as, for instance, when they say that He hath a devil, He, the Son of God!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see these things even now, and how men are many of them like serpents, both faithless and thankless, men who, viper-like, when they have enjoyed benefits and have been warmed by some, then they sting their benefactors?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This we have said, lest any should marvel, how, such signs having been wrought, they were not all converted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For behold, in our own times happened those (miracles) relating to the martyr Babylas, those relating to Jerusalem, those relating to the destruction of the temples, and not all were converted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why need I speak of ancient things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I have told you what happened last year; and none gave heed to it, but again little by little they fell off and sunk back.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The heaven stands perpetually crying aloud that it has a Master, and that it is the work of an Artificer, all this that we see—I mean the world—and yet some say that it is not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What happened to that Theodorus last year—whom did it not startle?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet nothing came of it, but having for a season become religious, they returned to the point from which they had started in their attempt to be religious.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So it was with the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is what the Prophet said of them: “When He slew them, then they sought Him, and turned early unto God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ps. lxxviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what need to speak of those things that are common to all?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many have fallen into diseases, how many have promised, if raised up, to work so great a change, and yet they have again become the same as ever!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This, if nothing else, shows that we have natural free-will—our changing all at once.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Were evil natural, this would not be: things that are natural and necessary, we cannot change from.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And yet,” you will say, “we do change from them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For do we not see some, who have the natural faculty to see, but are blinded by fear?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (True—) because this also is natural: * * if a different (necessity of) nature come not also into operation: (thus) it is natural to us, that being terrified we do not see; it is natural to us that when a greater fear supervenes, the other gives way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What then,” you will say, “if right-mindedness be indeed according to nature, but fear having overpowered it cast it out?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then if I shall show that some even then are not brought to a right mind, but even in these fears are reckless?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is this natural?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall I speak of ancient things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, of recent?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many in the midst of those fears continued laughing, mocking, and experienced nothing of the sort?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did not Pharaoh change immediately, and (as quickly) run back to his former wickedness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But here, as if (the demons) knew Him not, they (the exorcists) added, “Whom Paul preacheth,” whereas they ought to have said, “the Saviour of the world.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Him that rose again.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By this they show that they do know, but they did not choose to confess His glory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore the demon exposes them, leaping upon them, and saying, “Jesus I know, and Paul I know, but who are ye?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that not ye are believers, but ye abuse that Name when ye say this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore the Temple is desolate, the implement easy to be overcome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that ye are not preachers; mine, says he, ye are.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great was the wrath of the demon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Apostles had power to do this to them, but they did it not as yet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they that had power over the demons that did these things to them, much more had power over the men themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how their forbearance is shown, in that they whom they repulsed do these things, while the demons whom they courted do the contrary.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Jesus,” says he, “I know.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be ashamed, ye that are ignorant (of Him).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Paul I know.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well said, “Think not that it is because I despise them, that I do these things.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great was the fear of the demon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why without these words did he not rend their garments?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For so he would both have sated his wrath, and established the delusion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He feared as I said, the unapproachable force, and would not have had such power had he not said this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe how we find the demons everywhere more right minded (than the Jews), not daring to contradict nor accuse the Apostles, or Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There they say, “We know Thee who Thou art” (Matt. viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29); and, “Why art Thou come hither before the time to torment us” (Mark i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24): and again, “I know Thee who Thou art, the Son of God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And here, “These men are servants of the most high God” (ch. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17): and again, “Jesus I know, and Paul I know.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they exceedingly feared and trembled before those holy persons.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps some one of you, hearing of these things, wishes he were possessed of this power, so that the demons should not be able to look him in the face, and accounts those saints happy for this, that they had such power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let him hear Christ saying, “Rejoice not because the demons are subject unto you” (Luke x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20), because He knew that all men rejoice most in this, through vainglory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if thou seekest that which pleaseth God, and that which is for the common good, there is another, a greater way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not so great to free from a demon as it is to rescue from sin.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A demon hinders not to attain unto the kingdom of Heaven, nay, even cooperates, unwillingly indeed, but nevertheless cooperates by making him that has the demon more sober-minded; but sin casts a man out.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it is likely some man will say, “God forbid it should ever befall me to be sobered in this way!”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nor do I wish it for you, but a very different way, that you should do all from love of Christ: if however, which God forbid, it should so befall you, then even on this behalf I would comfort you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then the demon does not cast out (from the kingdom of heaven), but sin does cast out, to free a man from sin is greater beneficence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From this let us study to free our neighbors, and before our neighbors, our own selves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us see to it, lest we have a demon: let us examine ourselves strictly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: More grievous than a demon is sin, for the demon makes men humble.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See ye not those possessed with a demon, when they have recovered from the attack, how downcast they are, of how sad a countenance, how fraught with shame their faces are, how they have not even courage to look one in the face?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See the strange inconsistency!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: While those are ashamed on account of the things they suffer, we are not ashamed on account of the things we do; while they are abashed being wronged, we are not abashed when doing wrong: and yet their condition is not a subject for shame, but for pity and tenderness and indulgence: nay, great is the admiration it calls for, and many the praises, when struggling against such a spirit, they bear all thankfully: whereas our condition in very deed is a subject for ridicule, for shame, for accusation, for correction, for punishment, for the worst of evils, for hell-fire; calling for no compassion whatever.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou, that worse than a demon is sin?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And those indeed, from the ills they suffer, reap a double profit: first, their being sobered and brought to more self-control; then, that having suffered here the chastisement of their own sins, they depart hence to their Master, purified.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed upon this we have often discoursed to you, that those who are punished here, if they bear it thankfully, may naturally be supposed to put away thereby many of their sins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whereas from sins the mischief resulting is twofold; first, that we offend; secondly, that we become worse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Attend to what I say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not this is the only injury we get from sin, that we commit a sin: but another and a worse is this, that our soul receives a habit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as it is in the case of the body—for it will be more plain when put in the form of an example—as he who has taken a fever has got harm not only in this respect, that he is sick, but also that after the sickness he is become weaker, even though he may return to health after a long disease: just so in the case of sin, though we may regain health, yet we are far from having the strength we need.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For take the case of one who has been insolently abusive: does he not suffer his deserts for his abusive conduct?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Aye, but there is another and a worse thing to rue (which is), that his soul is become more insensible to shame.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For from each several sin that is committed, even after the sin has been done and has ceased, there remains a kind of venom instilled into our souls.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not hear people saying, when they are recovered from sickness, “I dare not drink water now?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet the man has regained his health: aye, but the disease has done him this harm also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And whereas those (possessed) persons, albeit suffering ill, are thankful, we, when faring well, blaspheme God, and think ourselves very ill used: for you will find more persons behaving thus in health and wealth than in poverty and sickness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there stands the demon over (the possessed), like a very hangman, fierce, uttering many (menaces), even as a schoolmaster brandishing the lash, and not suffering them to give way to any laxity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And suppose that some are not at all brought to a sober mind, neither are these liable to punishment; no small thing this: even as fools, even as madmen and children, are not called to account, so neither are these: since for things that are done in a state of unconsciousness, none can be so merciless as to call the doers to account.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, in a far worse condition than those who are possessed of evil sprits are we that sin.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We do not, indeed, foam at the mouth, nor distort our eyes, or throw about our hands convulsively; but as for this, would that we did it in our body and not in our soul!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Will you that I show you a soul, foaming, filthy, and a distortion of the mind’s eyes?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think of those who are in a passion and drunken with rage; can any form be filthier than the words they discharge?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In very deed it is like a sputtering of noisome slaver.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And just as the possessed know none of those who are present, so neither do these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Their understanding darkened, their eyes distorted, they see not who is friend, who foe, who worthy of respect, who contemptible, but they see all alike without a difference.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then, do you not see them, how they tremble, just like those others?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they do not fall to the ground, say you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, but their soul lies on the ground and falls there in convulsions: since had it stood upright, it would not have come into the condition it is in.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or think you not that it betokens a soul abjectly sprawling and lost to all self-possession, the things men can do and say when drunken with rage?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is also another form of madness worse than this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What may this be?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When men cannot so much as suffer themselves to vent their anger, but instead of that nourish within their own bosoms, to their own proper hurt, as it were a very hangman with his lash, the rancorous remembrance of wrongs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is a bane to themselves first, the malice that they bear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To say nothing of the things to come, what torture, think you, must that man undergo in the scourging of his soul, as day by day he looks how he may avenge himself on his enemy?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He chastises himself first, and suffers punishment, swelling (with suppressed passion), fighting against himself, setting himself on fire.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For needs must the fire be always burning within thee: while raising the fever to such a height, and not suffering it to wane, thou thinkest thou art inflicting some evil on the other, whereas thou art wasting thyself, ever bearing about with thee a flame which is always at its height, and not letting thy soul have rest, but evermore being in a state of fury, and having thy thoughts in a turmoil and tempest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is more grievous than this madness, to be always smarting with pain, and ever swelling and inflamed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For such are the souls of the resentful: when they see him on whom they wish to be revenged, straightway it is as if a blow were struck them: if they hear his voice, they cower and tremble: if they be on their bed, they picture to themselves numberless revenges, hanging, torturing that enemy of theirs: and if, beside all this, they see him also to be in renown, O! the misery they suffer!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Forgive him the offence, and free thyself from the torment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why continue always in a state of punishment, that thou mayest once punish him, and take thy revenge?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why establish for thyself a hectic disease?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, when thy wrath would fain depart from thee, dost thou keep it back?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let it not remain until the evening, says Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For like some eating rot or moth, even so does it gnaw through the very root of our understanding.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why shut up a beast within thy bowels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Better a serpent or an adder to lie within thy heart, than anger and resentment: for those indeed would soon have done with us, but this remains forever fixing in us its fangs, instilling its poison, letting loose upon us an invading host of bitter thoughts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That he should laugh me to scorn,” say you, “that he should despise me!”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O wretched, miserable man, wouldest thou not be ridiculed by thy fellow-servant, and wouldest thou be hated by thy Master?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wouldest thou not be despised by thy fellow-servant, and despisest thou thy Master?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To be despised by him, is it more than thou canst bear, but thinkest thou not that God is indignant, because thou ridiculest Him, because thou despisest Him, when thou wilt not do as He bids thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that thine enemy will not even ridicule thee, is manifest from hence (that), whereas if thou follow up the revenge, great is the ridicule, great the contempt, for this is a mark of a little mind; on the contrary, if thou forgive him, great is the admiration, for this is a mark of greatness of soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But you will say, he knows not this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let God know it, that thou mayest have the greater reward.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For He says, “Lend to those of whom ye hope not to receive.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So let us also do good to those who do not even perceive that one is doing them good, that they may not, by returning to us praise or any other thing, lessen our reward.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when we receive nothing from men, then we shall receive greater things from God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what is more worthy of ridicule, what more paltry, than a soul which is always in anger, and wishing to take revenge?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is womanly, this disposition, it is babyish.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as the babes are angry even with lifeless things, and unless the mother beats the ground, they will not let go their anger: so do these persons wish to revenge themselves on those who have aggrieved them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, it is they who are worthy of ridicule: for to be overcome by passion, is the mark of a childish understanding, but to overcome it, is a sign of manliness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, not we are the objects of ridicule, when we keep our temper, but they.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not this that makes men contemptible—not to be conquered by passion: what makes them contemptible is this—to be so afraid of ridicule from without, as on this account to choose to subject one’s self to one’s besetting passion, and to offend God, and take revenge upon one’s self.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things are indeed worthy of ridicule.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us flee them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let a man say, that having done us numberless ills, he has suffered nothing in return: let him say that he might again frantically assault us, and have nothing to fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, in no other (better) way could he have proclaimed our virtue; no other words would he have sought, if he had wished to praise us, than those which he seems to say in abuse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would that all men said these things of me: “he is a poor tame creature; all men heap insults on him, but he bears it: all men trample upon him, but he does not avenge himself.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would that they added, “neither, if he should wish to do so, can he:” that so I might have praise from God, and not from men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let him say, that it is for want of spirit that we do not avenge ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This does us no hurt, when God knows (all): it does but cause our treasure to be in greater safety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we are to have regard to them, we shall fall away from everything.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not look to what they say, but to what becomes us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, says he, “Let no man ridicule me,” and some make a boast of this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O! what folly!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “No man,” says he, “having injured me, has ridiculed me:” that is, “I had my revenge.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet for this thou deservest to be ridiculed, that thou didst take revenge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence came these words among us—being, as they are, a disgrace to us and a pest, an overthrow of our own proper life and of our discipline?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is in downright opposition to God that thou (so) speakest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The very thing which makes thee equal to God—the not avenging thyself—this thou thinkest a subject for ridicule!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are not we for these things worthy to be laughed at, both by ourselves, and by the heathen, when we thus speak against God?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I wish to tell you a story of a thing that happened in the old times (which they tell) not on the subject of anger, but of money.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A man had an estate in which there was a hidden treasure, unknown to the owner: this piece of ground he sold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The buyer, when digging it for the purpose of planting and cultivation, found the treasure therein deposited, and came and wanted to oblige the seller to receive the treasure, urging that he had bought a piece of ground, not a treasure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The seller on his part repudiated the gift, saying, “The piece of ground (is not mine), I have sold it, and I have no concern whatever with this (treasure).”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So they fell to altercation about it, the one wishing to give it, the other standing out against receiving it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So chancing upon some third person, they argued the matter before him, and said to him, “To whom ought the treasure to be assigned?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man could not settle that question; he said, however, that he would put an end to their dispute—he would (if they pleased) be master of it himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So he received the treasure, which they willingly gave up to him; and in the sequel got into troubles without end, and learnt by actual experience that they had done well to have nothing to do with it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So ought it be done likewise with regard to anger; both ourselves ought to be emulous not to take revenge, and those who have aggrieved us, emulous to give satisfaction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But perhaps these things also seem to be matter of ridicule: for when that madness is widely prevalent among men, those who keep their temper are laughed at, and among many madmen he who is not a madman seems to be mad.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore I beseech you that we may recover (from this malady), and come to our senses, that becoming pure from this pernicious passion, we may be enabled to attain unto the kingdom of heaven, through the grace and mercy of His only-begotten Son, with Whom to the Father, together with the Holy Spirit, be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
